stimulus: A city installed bright new streetlights along its busiest commercial corridor last year, expecting the well-lit environment to deter criminal activity. Yet police records show that reported thefts in that corridor have increased by 30 percent since the lights were installed, even though the overall crime rate citywide has remained stable.
stem: Which one of the following, if true, most helps to resolve the apparent discrepancy described above?
choice_a: The new streetlights consume less energy than the old ones they replaced.
choice_b: The brighter lighting makes it easier for victims and bystanders to observe and report thefts that previously went unnoticed in darker conditions.
choice_c: Several other corridors in the city also received new streetlights during the same period.
choice_d: The police department hired additional officers to patrol the commercial corridor after the lights were installed.
choice_e: Most thefts in the corridor occur during daytime business hours rather than at night.
explanation: The paradox is that better lighting was expected to reduce theft but reported thefts increased. Choice B resolves this by explaining that the lighting didn't increase actual theft — it increased the ability to detect and report theft that was already occurring. The apparent rise is in reporting, not in criminal activity itself.
explanation_a: Energy consumption is irrelevant to theft rates. This does not address the discrepancy.
explanation_b: Correct. If brighter lights help people see and report thefts that previously went unnoticed, reported thefts would rise even if actual thefts stayed the same or decreased.
explanation_c: Other corridors receiving lights does not explain why this corridor's reported thefts rose.
explanation_d: Additional officers would be expected to deter crime further, which deepens rather than resolves the paradox.
explanation_e: If most thefts occur in daytime, streetlights at night would be irrelevant, but this doesn't explain why reported thefts increased after installation.

stimulus: Monarch butterflies are known to be highly toxic to birds because of the milkweed they consume as caterpillars. Despite this toxicity, researchers have found that certain species of birds in the monarchs' wintering grounds in central Mexico regularly prey on monarchs, consuming thousands of them each season without apparent ill effects.
stem: Which one of the following, if true, most helps to explain the situation described above?
choice_a: Monarch butterflies that winter in Mexico have migrated thousands of miles from the United States and Canada.
choice_b: These bird species have evolved a genetic variation that makes them resistant to the specific toxins found in milkweed.
choice_c: The birds in question also feed on many other insect species during the winter months.
choice_d: Monarch populations in the wintering grounds have been declining in recent decades.
choice_e: The toxicity of monarchs varies depending on which species of milkweed the caterpillars consumed.
explanation: The paradox is that monarchs are toxic to birds, yet certain birds eat them without ill effects. Choice B directly resolves this by explaining that these particular bird species have evolved resistance to the toxin, so the poison simply doesn't affect them.
explanation_a: The distance of migration does not explain why these birds can eat toxic butterflies without harm.
explanation_b: Correct. Genetic resistance to the milkweed toxins would allow these birds to consume monarchs without suffering ill effects, directly resolving the discrepancy.
explanation_c: Eating other insects doesn't explain why these birds survive eating toxic monarchs.
explanation_d: Declining monarch populations do not explain why birds can eat them without being harmed.
explanation_e: While toxicity variation is relevant, it doesn't fully explain why the birds show no apparent ill effects at all despite eating thousands.

stimulus: A large law firm recently implemented a policy requiring all associates to limit their billable hours to no more than fifty per week, aiming to reduce burnout and improve retention. However, in the six months following the policy's adoption, associate turnover actually increased compared to the same period in the prior year.
stem: Which one of the following, if true, most helps to resolve the apparent discrepancy described above?
choice_a: The firm's competitors did not implement similar billable hour restrictions during the same period.
choice_b: Associates who left cited the reduction in billable hours as decreasing their annual bonus compensation to unacceptable levels.
choice_c: The firm also hired a significant number of new associates during the six-month period.
choice_d: Several partners at the firm publicly opposed the new policy.
choice_e: Burnout among associates had been increasing for several years prior to the policy change.
explanation: The paradox is that a policy meant to reduce burnout and improve retention actually increased turnover. Choice B explains this by showing that the policy had an unintended consequence: reducing billable hours also reduced bonus compensation, giving associates a financial reason to leave.
explanation_a: Competitors not having similar policies doesn't directly explain why turnover increased at this firm.
explanation_b: Correct. If the hour cap reduced bonuses, associates had a new financial motivation to leave, which outweighed the burnout reduction benefit.
explanation_c: Hiring new associates doesn't explain why existing associates left at higher rates.
explanation_d: Partner opposition might create tension but doesn't directly explain why associates chose to leave.
explanation_e: Increasing burnout before the policy would suggest the policy was needed, not why it backfired.

stimulus: A publishing house found that its audiobook sales have tripled over the past three years. Surprisingly, during the same period, sales of the same titles in print format have also increased substantially rather than declining, even though the total number of book buyers in the market has remained essentially unchanged.
stem: Which one of the following, if true, most helps to explain the situation described above?
choice_a: Audiobook production costs have decreased significantly due to advances in recording technology.
choice_b: Many audiobook listeners purchase the print version of the same title so they can revisit specific passages more easily.
choice_c: The publishing house has expanded its catalog of available titles during the past three years.
choice_d: Bookstores have been closing at an increasing rate across the country.
choice_e: The average price of audiobooks has remained stable over the three-year period.
explanation: The paradox is that audiobook sales increased but print sales also rose despite no new buyers entering the market. Choice B resolves this by showing that audiobook listeners are also buying the print version, meaning the same buyers are purchasing more units across formats.
explanation_a: Lower production costs explain why more audiobooks are produced but not why print sales also rose.
explanation_b: Correct. If audiobook listeners also buy the print version, total purchases per buyer increase, explaining how both formats grew without new buyers.
explanation_c: An expanded catalog might explain overall sales growth but doesn't specifically explain why both formats grew simultaneously with no new buyers.
explanation_d: Bookstore closings would more likely hurt print sales, deepening the paradox rather than resolving it.
explanation_e: Stable audiobook prices don't explain the increase in print sales.

stimulus: A pharmaceutical company's clinical trial found that patients who took a new pain medication reported significantly less pain than those who took a placebo. Yet brain imaging conducted during the trial showed no measurable difference in pain-related neural activity between the two groups.
stem: Which one of the following, if true, most helps to resolve the apparent discrepancy described above?
choice_a: The medication was administered in pill form identical in appearance to the placebo.
choice_b: Patients in the medication group were slightly older on average than those in the placebo group.
choice_c: Some patients in the placebo group reported a slight decrease in pain during the trial.
choice_d: The brain imaging technology used in the study is considered highly accurate and reliable.
choice_e: The medication affects how patients emotionally interpret pain signals rather than reducing the neural activity that generates those signals.
explanation: The paradox is that patients reported less pain, but brain imaging showed the same pain-related neural activity. Choice B explains this by distinguishing between pain signal generation and emotional interpretation — the drug changes how patients experience pain without altering the underlying neural signals.
explanation_a: Identical appearance is standard in clinical trials and doesn't explain the discrepancy between reported pain and neural activity.
explanation_b: Age difference is a confounding variable but doesn't explain the specific disconnect between reported pain and neural activity.
explanation_c: A slight placebo effect in the control group doesn't explain the discrepancy for the treatment group.
explanation_d: Reliable imaging technology makes the discrepancy more puzzling, not less.
explanation_e: Correct. If the drug modifies emotional processing of pain rather than the pain signals themselves, patients could report less pain while neural pain activity remains unchanged.

stimulus: A museum that recently began offering free admission on the first Sunday of each month expected the policy to attract visitors who otherwise could not afford the entrance fee. Attendance data shows that while overall Sunday attendance doubled on free days, the proportion of visitors from lower-income neighborhoods actually decreased compared to regular-price Sundays.
stem: Which one of the following, if true, most helps to resolve the apparent discrepancy described above?
choice_a: The museum is located in a wealthy downtown area with limited public transportation connections to lower-income neighborhoods.
choice_b: Other museums in the city do not offer free admission days.
choice_c: The museum's regular admission price is fifteen dollars per adult.
choice_d: Free admission days attracted such large crowds that visitors from lower-income neighborhoods, who tend to rely on public transit with fixed schedules, could not gain entry before capacity limits were reached.
choice_e: The museum increased its marketing budget to promote the free admission program.
explanation: The paradox is that free admission was meant to attract lower-income visitors, but their proportion decreased. Choice D explains that while free days drew large crowds, capacity limits and transit constraints disadvantaged lower-income visitors who couldn't arrive as early or flexibly as wealthier visitors with cars.
explanation_a: Limited transit access is a general barrier, but it would affect both free and paid days equally, so it doesn't explain why the proportion changed specifically on free days.
explanation_b: Other museums' policies don't explain the shift in visitor demographics on free days.
explanation_c: The admission price provides context but doesn't explain the proportional decrease.
explanation_d: Correct. If free days hit capacity quickly and lower-income visitors relying on fixed transit schedules arrived after capacity was reached, they would be disproportionately excluded from free days.
explanation_e: Increased marketing would be expected to reach all demographics and doesn't explain the proportional shift.

stimulus: Renaissance painter Artemisia Gentileschi was largely forgotten for centuries after her death, with her works frequently misattributed to male contemporaries. Despite this obscurity, auction records show that paintings later confirmed as hers sold for consistently high prices throughout the eighteenth and nineteenth centuries.
stem: Which one of the following, if true, most helps to explain the situation described above?
choice_a: Gentileschi's paintings employ unusually vivid colors that have remained vibrant over the centuries.
choice_b: Gentileschi received several prestigious commissions during her lifetime from European royalty.
choice_c: Art historians in the twentieth century began systematically re-examining attributions of Renaissance paintings.
choice_d: The paintings sold at high prices precisely because they were attributed to well-known male painters whose works commanded premium valuations.
choice_e: Many Renaissance paintings of uncertain authorship sell for relatively modest prices at auction.
explanation: The paradox is that Gentileschi was forgotten yet her paintings sold for high prices. Choice B resolves this neatly: the paintings fetched high prices because they were misattributed to famous male painters, so buyers were paying for the perceived prestige of a different artist's name.
explanation_a: Vivid colors might contribute to value but don't explain why a forgotten artist's works sold for high prices specifically.
explanation_b: Royal commissions during her lifetime don't explain high auction prices in centuries when she was forgotten.
explanation_c: Twentieth-century re-examination explains how her authorship was recovered but not why the paintings sold for high prices in earlier centuries.
explanation_d: Correct. If the works were attributed to famous male contemporaries, they would command high prices based on those attributions, even though Gentileschi herself was forgotten.
explanation_e: This choice actually deepens the paradox by suggesting unknown works sell for less.

stimulus: A state recently doubled the penalties for driving under the influence, expecting the harsher sentences to reduce drunk driving incidents. In the year following the change, the number of DUI arrests dropped by 40 percent, yet alcohol-related traffic fatalities remained virtually unchanged.
stem: Which one of the following, if true, most helps to resolve the apparent discrepancy described above?
choice_a: Neighboring states did not change their DUI penalties during the same period.
choice_b: The state also increased funding for public awareness campaigns about drunk driving.
choice_c: Police officers, aware that convictions now carry much harsher consequences, became more reluctant to arrest drivers for borderline DUI cases.
choice_d: The new penalties include mandatory installation of ignition interlock devices on offenders' vehicles.
choice_e: Alcohol sales in the state remained stable during the year following the penalty increase.
explanation: The paradox is that DUI arrests dropped but fatalities didn't. Choice B explains this: officers made fewer arrests not because fewer people were driving drunk, but because officers were reluctant to impose severe penalties on borderline cases. The actual drunk driving behavior didn't change, so fatalities stayed the same.
explanation_a: Neighboring states' policies don't explain the internal discrepancy between arrests and fatalities.
explanation_b: Awareness campaigns would be expected to reduce both arrests and fatalities, not create a divergence.
explanation_c: Correct. If officers arrest fewer borderline cases due to harsher penalties, arrest numbers fall while actual drunk driving continues at similar rates, keeping fatalities stable.
explanation_d: Ignition interlocks would further reduce drunk driving, deepening the paradox about unchanged fatalities.
explanation_e: Stable alcohol sales provide background information but don't explain the specific disconnect between arrests and fatalities.

stimulus: A recent study of a large tropical forest found that trees growing in areas with the richest, most nutrient-dense soil were significantly shorter and had thinner trunks than trees of the same species growing in areas with poor, nutrient-depleted soil nearby.
stem: Which one of the following, if true, most helps to explain the situation described above?
choice_a: Trees in tropical forests generally grow faster than trees in temperate forests.
choice_b: In nutrient-rich soil, trees invest less energy in root growth and more in leaf production.
choice_c: Nutrient-rich areas support denser vegetation, creating intense competition for sunlight that limits individual tree growth, while trees in poor soil face less competition and can grow larger.
choice_d: The nutrient-depleted soil areas receive slightly more annual rainfall.
choice_e: Some tropical tree species are better adapted to nutrient-poor soils than others.
explanation: The paradox is that trees in better soil are smaller. Choice C explains that nutrient-rich soil supports more plants overall, creating fierce competition that limits each individual tree's size. In poor soil, fewer plants survive, so those that do face less competition and grow larger.
explanation_a: A comparison with temperate forests is irrelevant to the within-forest discrepancy.
explanation_b: Investing in leaves over roots doesn't directly explain smaller height and thinner trunks.
explanation_c: Correct. Greater density of vegetation in rich soil creates competition for sunlight, limiting individual tree size. Poor soil means fewer competitors and more resources per surviving tree.
explanation_d: Slightly more rainfall in one area is a weak explanation and doesn't clearly account for the size difference.
explanation_e: Species adaptation to poor soil doesn't explain why the same species are larger in poor soil.

stimulus: A sociological study found that residents of a small rural town reported significantly higher levels of life satisfaction than residents of a nearby affluent suburb, despite the fact that the rural town had substantially lower average household income, fewer amenities, and less access to healthcare.
stem: Which one of the following, if true, most helps to explain the situation described above?
choice_a: The rural town has a lower cost of living than the affluent suburb.
choice_b: The affluent suburb has better-funded public schools than the rural town.
choice_c: The rural town's close-knit community structure provides residents with strong social bonds and mutual support networks that residents of the suburb, who report high levels of social isolation, lack.
choice_d: Many residents of the rural town have lived there for multiple generations.
choice_e: The suburb was developed within the last twenty years.
explanation: The paradox is that residents with fewer material advantages report higher life satisfaction. Choice C resolves this by identifying a specific factor — strong social bonds — that contributes greatly to life satisfaction and is present in the rural town but absent in the suburb.
explanation_a: Lower cost of living helps but doesn't fully explain higher satisfaction given the many other disadvantages listed.
explanation_b: Better schools in the suburb deepens the paradox rather than resolving it.
explanation_c: Correct. Strong community bonds and low social isolation can outweigh material disadvantages in determining life satisfaction, while suburban isolation undermines the benefits of affluence.
explanation_d: Multi-generational residence is interesting context but doesn't directly explain the satisfaction gap.
explanation_e: The suburb's age doesn't directly explain the life satisfaction difference.

stimulus: A medieval history scholar discovered that a fourteenth-century monastery known for its strict vow of poverty possessed an extraordinarily large and richly illuminated library. The library contained hundreds of manuscripts decorated with gold leaf and expensive pigments, apparently contradicting the monks' ascetic commitments.
stem: Which one of the following, if true, most helps to resolve the apparent discrepancy described above?
choice_a: The monastery's vow of poverty applied to individual possessions; communal property held for the glory of God, including devotional manuscripts, was specifically exempt.
choice_b: Other monasteries of the same period also maintained substantial libraries.
choice_c: The monastery also maintained extensive agricultural lands.
choice_d: Gold leaf was less expensive in the fourteenth century than it is today.
choice_e: Many of the manuscripts were written in Latin rather than the local vernacular.
explanation: The paradox is that monks sworn to poverty possessed lavish manuscripts. Choice B resolves this by explaining that the vow applied to personal, not communal, property, and devotional manuscripts were classified as communal property for worship, not personal luxury.
explanation_a: Correct. If the vow of poverty exempted communal devotional property, the library does not violate the monks' ascetic commitments.
explanation_b: Other monasteries having libraries doesn't explain why this poverty-sworn monastery had one.
explanation_c: Agricultural lands raise a separate question and don't explain the library.
explanation_d: Even if gold leaf was cheaper, spending on luxury items still conflicts with a vow of poverty.
explanation_e: The language of manuscripts is irrelevant to the apparent conflict with the poverty vow.

stimulus: A country passed a comprehensive whistleblower protection law designed to encourage employees to report corporate fraud. In the two years after the law took effect, the number of whistleblower complaints filed with regulators actually decreased by 15 percent compared to the two years before the law.
stem: Which one of the following, if true, most helps to resolve the apparent discrepancy described above?
choice_a: The law imposed significant fines on companies found to have retaliated against whistleblowers.
choice_b: Corporate fraud rates have generally been increasing worldwide.
choice_c: Public awareness of the new whistleblower protections was high due to extensive media coverage.
choice_d: The law also extended protections to whistleblowers in government agencies.
choice_e: After the law was passed, companies began establishing robust internal compliance programs, resolving many issues before employees felt the need to file external complaints.
explanation: The paradox is that stronger protections for whistleblowers led to fewer complaints. Choice B explains that the law's deterrent effect caused companies to proactively address fraud internally, reducing the situations that would generate external whistleblower complaints.
explanation_a: Fines for retaliation strengthen protections, which should encourage more complaints, deepening the paradox.
explanation_b: Increasing fraud worldwide makes the decrease in complaints even more surprising.
explanation_c: High public awareness should increase complaints, making the decrease more puzzling.
explanation_d: Extending protections to government employees should increase total complaints, not decrease them.
explanation_e: Correct. If companies created internal compliance programs in response to the law, fraud was addressed internally before reaching the point of external whistleblower complaints.

stimulus: Studies show that professional translators who are fluent in three or more languages consistently produce lower-quality translations than equally experienced translators who are fluent in only two languages. This finding holds even when both groups are translating between the same pair of languages.
stem: Which one of the following, if true, most helps to explain the situation described above?
choice_a: Multilingual translators command higher fees in the translation industry.
choice_b: Bilingual translators tend to have more formal training in translation methodology.
choice_c: Some languages are more difficult to translate between than others.
choice_d: Knowledge of additional languages creates interference effects, causing multilingual translators to inadvertently incorporate vocabulary or grammatical structures from their other languages into the translation.
choice_e: Machine translation tools have improved significantly in recent years.
explanation: The paradox is that knowing more languages leads to worse translations. Choice B explains this through linguistic interference — additional languages create cognitive cross-contamination that degrades translation quality between any specific pair.
explanation_a: Higher fees don't explain lower quality — if anything, they suggest the market expects higher quality.
explanation_b: This would be an alternative explanation, but it attributes the difference to training rather than multilingualism itself and is less directly tied to the paradox about language fluency.
explanation_c: Translation difficulty between specific languages is controlled for in the study, since both groups translate the same pair.
explanation_d: Correct. Interference from additional languages explains why multilingual translators produce lower-quality work even when translating between the same language pair.
explanation_e: Machine translation tools are irrelevant to the comparison between human translators.

stimulus: A national park service introduced a modest entrance fee at a previously free nature reserve, expecting some decline in visitation. Total annual visitors did decrease slightly. However, the park experienced significantly more environmental damage — including trampled vegetation, littering, and trail erosion — after the fee was introduced.
stem: Which one of the following, if true, most helps to resolve the apparent discrepancy described above?
choice_a: The entrance fee revenue was used to hire additional park maintenance staff.
choice_b: Other national parks charge higher entrance fees without experiencing similar problems.
choice_c: Visitors who paid the fee felt entitled to use the park however they wished and were less likely to follow conservation guidelines, while the fee deterred the environmentally conscious visitors who had previously been the park's most careful users.
choice_d: The nature reserve contains several species listed as threatened or endangered.
choice_e: Annual rainfall in the area was slightly above average during the year the fee was introduced.
explanation: The paradox is that fewer visitors caused more environmental damage. Choice B explains this through a selection effect: the fee drove away careful, environmentally conscious visitors while those who remained felt their payment entitled them to less responsible behavior.
explanation_a: More maintenance staff should reduce damage, making this answer deepen the paradox.
explanation_b: Other parks' experiences don't explain this park's specific situation.
explanation_c: Correct. The fee changed the composition and behavior of visitors — careful visitors left, remaining visitors felt entitled — explaining more damage despite fewer total visitors.
explanation_d: Endangered species are relevant context but don't explain the increased damage.
explanation_e: Above-average rainfall might affect trail erosion but doesn't explain trampling and littering.

stimulus: A well-documented volcanic eruption in 1815 ejected massive amounts of ash and sulfur dioxide into the atmosphere, causing global temperatures to drop. Counterintuitively, historical records from the following year show that some regions in the Northern Hemisphere experienced their hottest summer days on record alongside unprecedented cold snaps within the same season.
stem: Which one of the following, if true, most helps to explain the situation described above?
choice_a: The ash cloud disrupted normal atmospheric circulation patterns, causing some regions to receive prolonged exposure to stagnant hot air masses interspersed with sudden incursions of arctic air, producing extreme variability rather than uniformly cooler temperatures.
choice_b: The volcanic eruption was one of the largest in recorded history.
choice_c: Volcanic eruptions typically produce temporary cooling that lasts for two to three years.
choice_d: Agricultural output declined significantly in many Northern Hemisphere countries the following year.
choice_e: Average global temperatures in the year following the eruption were below the historical mean.
explanation: The paradox is that a cooling event produced both record heat and unprecedented cold in the same season. Choice B explains that the volcanic debris disrupted atmospheric circulation, causing extreme weather variability — stagnant heat domes and sudden cold intrusions — rather than uniform cooling.
explanation_a: Correct. Disrupted circulation explains how global cooling can produce local temperature extremes in both directions within the same season.
explanation_b: The eruption's size provides context but doesn't explain the contradictory temperature extremes.
explanation_c: Duration of cooling doesn't address why both hot and cold extremes occurred simultaneously.
explanation_d: Agricultural decline is a consequence of the temperature anomalies, not an explanation for them.
explanation_e: Below-average global temperatures are consistent with cooling but don't explain the localized heat records.

stimulus: A jurisdiction changed its jury selection rules to require that jury pools more closely reflect the demographic composition of the community. Proponents expected this to increase public trust in the judicial system. Surveys conducted one year later, however, showed that public trust in jury verdicts had actually declined since the new rules took effect.
stem: Which one of the following, if true, most helps to resolve the apparent discrepancy described above?
choice_a: The new jury selection rules were upheld as constitutional by an appellate court.
choice_b: Similar jury selection reforms in other jurisdictions had mixed results.
choice_c: Jury duty compensation was not increased when the new rules were implemented.
choice_d: The average length of jury trials remained the same after the rule change.
choice_e: Extensive media coverage of the demographic changes led many citizens to believe that jurors were being selected based on identity rather than impartiality, undermining confidence in the fairness of verdicts.
explanation: The paradox is that more representative juries decreased public trust. Choice B explains that media framing of the changes made people perceive jury selection as identity-based rather than merit-based, which undermined the very trust the reforms sought to build.
explanation_a: Constitutional validity doesn't affect public perception of fairness.
explanation_b: Mixed results elsewhere don't explain the specific mechanism causing decline here.
explanation_c: Compensation levels don't explain the trust decline.
explanation_d: Trial length is irrelevant to public trust in verdicts.
explanation_e: Correct. If the public perceived the demographic focus as prioritizing identity over impartiality, trust would decline even though the juries were more representative.

stimulus: A technology company that switched from individual performance bonuses to team-based bonuses expected collaboration to improve. Managers reported that teamwork did increase substantially. Yet the company's overall productivity, as measured by output per employee, declined by 12 percent in the year following the switch.
stem: Which one of the following, if true, most helps to resolve the apparent discrepancy described above?
choice_a: The company's main competitor experienced a similar productivity decline during the same period.
choice_b: The company also introduced new project management software during the same period.
choice_c: Employee satisfaction surveys showed improved morale after the switch.
choice_d: Team-based bonuses reduced the incentive for top performers to maximize their individual output, since their extra effort was diluted across the entire team's shared reward.
choice_e: Some teams chose to distribute their bonuses equally regardless of individual contribution.
explanation: The paradox is that improved teamwork coincided with reduced productivity. Choice B explains this through a free-rider problem: top performers reduced their individual effort because the team-based structure diluted their personal reward for extra output.
explanation_a: A competitor's decline might suggest a market-wide trend but doesn't explain the specific link between team bonuses and lower productivity.
explanation_b: New software is a confounding factor but doesn't explain the specific connection to the bonus structure change.
explanation_c: Improved morale makes the productivity decline more puzzling, not less.
explanation_d: Correct. When top performers' extra effort is diluted across a team bonus, they rationally reduce their output, lowering overall productivity even as collaboration improves.
explanation_e: Equal distribution of bonuses is a detail about implementation but doesn't directly explain the productivity decline mechanism.

stimulus: Historians have long noted that the most prolific period of classical Athenian theater — producing works still performed today — occurred during an era when Athens was embroiled in devastating military conflicts and suffered a catastrophic plague. One would expect that such hardships would suppress artistic output rather than fuel it.
stem: Which one of the following, if true, most helps to explain the situation described above?
choice_a: Theater provided Athenians with a communal space to process collective trauma, grief, and moral questions raised by war and plague, giving playwrights an urgent and receptive audience for their work.
choice_b: Athenian theater performances were subsidized by wealthy citizens as a civic duty.
choice_c: Athens was a democracy during this period, and citizens participated in government decisions.
choice_d: Greek tragedy originated in religious festivals dedicated to the god Dionysus.
choice_e: Many Athenian playwrights also served in the military during the conflicts.
explanation: The paradox is that crisis fueled rather than suppressed artistic output. Choice B explains that suffering created both the emotional material and the audience demand for theater as a means of processing collective experience.
explanation_a: Correct. If crises created urgent need for communal processing through theater, playwrights had both compelling subject matter and eager audiences, explaining the creative peak.
explanation_b: Subsidies explain funding but not why artistic quality and output peaked during hardship specifically.
explanation_c: Democracy is background context that doesn't explain the paradox.
explanation_d: Religious origins of theater are historical background, not an explanation for the timing of the peak.
explanation_e: Military service by playwrights is interesting but doesn't explain why overall theatrical output increased during hardship.

stimulus: A study of deep-sea ecosystems found thriving communities of organisms at hydrothermal vents on the ocean floor. These ecosystems are as productive as tropical coral reefs. Yet the deep sea surrounding these vents receives virtually no sunlight, which is the energy source that supports nearly all other known ecosystems on Earth.
stem: Which one of the following, if true, most helps to explain the situation described above?
choice_a: Hydrothermal vents are found along tectonic plate boundaries throughout the world's oceans.
choice_b: Deep-sea ecosystems were not discovered until the late twentieth century.
choice_c: Some deep-sea organisms have adaptations that allow them to survive under extreme pressure.
choice_d: The water temperature near hydrothermal vents can exceed 300 degrees Celsius.
choice_e: Specialized bacteria at the vents convert chemical energy from hydrogen sulfide and other minerals into organic compounds through chemosynthesis, providing the base of the food chain without sunlight.
explanation: The paradox is that productive ecosystems exist without sunlight. Choice B explains that chemosynthetic bacteria use chemical energy instead of solar energy, providing an alternative energy foundation for the food chain.
explanation_a: The global distribution of vents doesn't explain how life thrives without sunlight.
explanation_b: The date of discovery is irrelevant to how the ecosystems function.
explanation_c: Pressure adaptations explain survival at depth but not the energy source for the ecosystem.
explanation_d: Extreme temperatures are a challenge organisms must overcome, not an explanation for ecosystem productivity.
explanation_e: Correct. Chemosynthesis replaces photosynthesis as the energy source, explaining how a productive ecosystem functions without sunlight.

stimulus: A legal aid organization began offering free consultations to tenants facing eviction, expecting to help more tenants successfully fight their cases. After the program launched, the number of tenants who formally contested their evictions in court actually dropped by 25 percent, even though the number seeking consultations was high.
stem: Which one of the following, if true, most helps to resolve the apparent discrepancy described above?
choice_a: During consultations, attorneys often informed tenants that their cases lacked legal merit, leading those tenants to negotiate settlements or relocate voluntarily rather than pursue futile court battles.
choice_b: The legal aid organization also provides assistance with other types of civil cases.
choice_c: Eviction rates in the city have been rising for several years.
choice_d: The organization employs attorneys who specialize in landlord-tenant law.
choice_e: Tenants who contested their evictions before the program had a low success rate in court.
explanation: The paradox is that free legal help reduced court contests. Choice B explains that consultations gave tenants realistic assessments — many learned their cases were weak and chose more practical alternatives, reducing the number of formal court challenges.
explanation_a: Correct. Professional legal advice helped tenants understand when contesting was futile, leading them to settle or relocate instead of going to court.
explanation_b: The organization's other services are irrelevant to the eviction contest rate.
explanation_c: Rising eviction rates don't explain why consultations reduced court contests.
explanation_d: Attorney specialization doesn't explain why consultations reduced formal court challenges.
explanation_e: Low prior success rates provide context but don't explain the mechanism by which consultations reduced contests.
